notes: FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 48 cal/100g, 9g sugar/100g. Compare to dried apricot at 241 cal/100g — 5x calorie concentration.
FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 39 cal/100g, 8g sugar/100g. Compare to dried peach at 239 cal/100g — 6x calorie concentration.
FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 60 cal/100g, 14g sugar/100g. Compare to dried mango at 314 cal/100g — 5x calorie concentration.
FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 69 cal/100g, 16g sugar/100g. Compare to raisins at 299 cal/100g — 4.3x calorie concentration.
FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 46 cal/100g, only 4g sugar/100g. Craisins add 26g sugar per 40g serving — almost ALL added sugar since raw cranberries are very tart.
FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 50 cal/100g, 10g sugar/100g. Compare to dried pineapple at 325 cal/100g — 6.5x calorie concentration.
FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 89 cal/100g. Compare to banana chips (FRIED) at 519 cal/100g — 5.8x concentration. Fried banana chips have MORE calories than potato chips!
FRESH FRUIT BASELINE. 50 cal/100g, 9g sugar/100g. Compare to dried cherries at 325 cal/100g — 6.5x calorie concentration.

THE CLASSIC calorie trap. 306 cal/100g, 71g sugar/100g. That tiny box = 130 cal of pure sugar. Fresh grapes are 69 cal/100g — raisins are 4.4x more calorie-dense. People pour these on salads thinking they're healthy.
275 cal/100g, 38g sugar/100g. Fresh apricots are only 48 cal/100g — dried are 5.7x more concentrated. Marketed as 'healthy snack' but calorically dense.
250 cal/100g, 45g sugar/100g. Similar to Mariani. The serving looks tiny — 6 small apricots = 100 cal. Easy to eat 10-15 while snacking = 250+ cal.
300 cal/100g, 48g sugar/100g. Premium California apricots — slightly higher sugar than Turkish varieties. Still 6x more calorically dense than fresh apricots.
350 cal/100g, 70g sugar/100g. Sweetened dried mango — most brands add sugar ON TOP of already-sweet dried mango. Fresh mango is 60 cal/100g — these are nearly 6x more dense.
375 cal/100g, 75g sugar/100g. THE most popular dried mango brand. Heavily sweetened — 75% sugar by weight. That bag disappears fast and you've eaten 500+ cal without realizing it.
300 cal/100g, 55g sugar/100g. Unsweetened = no ADDED sugar, but still very high in natural fruit sugar. Better than sweetened (375 cal/100g) but still a calorie trap.
375 cal/100g, 70g sugar/100g. Added sugar makes this essentially candy with mango flavor. The sweetened vs unsweetened choice matters: 25% more calories.

MASSIVE TRAP. 325 cal/100g, 73g sugar/100g. Raw cranberries have only 4g sugar/100g — Craisins add ~26g of sugar per serving. That's as much added sugar as a Snickers bar. People sprinkle these on salads thinking they're healthy fruit.
250 cal/100g, 45g sugar/100g. '50% less sugar' than original — still 18g sugar per tiny 1/4 cup. Better but still not great. Uses sucralose to replace some sugar.
231 cal/100g, 38g sugar/100g. Among the BETTER dried fruits — high fiber, moderate sugar. But still 3-4x more calorie-dense than fresh plums (46 cal/100g). Often eaten for digestive health.

NATURE'S CANDY. 292 cal/100g, 63g sugar/100g. Two dates = 140 cal, 30g sugar. People eat these as a 'healthy dessert' but they're calorie-dense. Fitness influencer favorite for 'energy balls' — adding nut butter to dates creates 400+ cal snacks.
250 cal/100g, 48g sugar/100g, but 10g fiber/100g — highest fiber of any dried fruit. Still 3.4x more dense than fresh figs (74 cal/100g). The fiber makes them slightly more filling.

BIGGEST TRAP IN THE CATEGORY. 519 cal/100g, 33g fat/100g — they're DEEP FRIED in coconut oil. More calories than potato chips (536 cal/100g)! Fresh bananas are 89 cal/100g. People think 'banana = healthy' but these are essentially chips. Always check: if they're golden and crunchy, they're fried.
325 cal/100g, 65g sugar/100g. Like Craisins — tart cherries need added sugar to taste good dried. Fresh cherries are 50 cal/100g — dried are 6.5x more dense.
325 cal/100g, 68g sugar/100g. Fresh pineapple is only 50 cal/100g — dried is 6.5x more dense. The rings look small but are solid sugar. Often has added sugar on top of naturally sweet pineapple.
239 cal/100g, 42g sugar/100g. Fresh peaches are 39 cal/100g — dried are 6.1x more dense. Among the lower-calorie dried fruits but still a trap at volume.

BETTER ALTERNATIVE. 350 cal/100g — same as dried fruit per 100g! BUT the serving weighs only 10g (vs 40g for dried fruit) because freeze-dried is ultra-light and airy. You get a full cup for 35 cal. The volume tricks your brain into feeling satisfied.
350 cal/100g, 68g sugar/100g. This is their DRIED (not freeze-dried) mango — same calorie trap as other dried mangoes. Don't confuse with freeze-dried versions.
294 cal/100g. Kroger store brand freeze-dried. The whole bag is only 100 cal — great portion-controlled snack. Compare to a 40g bag of regular dried fruit at 130+ cal.
413 cal/100g. Higher cal/100g than regular dried mango because mango has more sugar than berries. But the whole bag is light and airy — you get way more volume than dried mango for similar calories.
389 cal/100g on paper, BUT a serving is only 9g = 35 cal! You get a decent handful for almost nothing. This is the freeze-dried advantage — same concentration but you eat far less weight.
400 cal/100g but only 40 cal per bag. Single-ingredient air-dried mango. Nothing added. The bag looks full but weighs almost nothing — great for volume snacking.
350 cal/100g but only 35 cal per bag. Lowest-calorie option in the freeze/air-dried category. Apple flavor, satisfying crunch.
400 cal/100g, 0g fat. Compare to FRIED banana chips at 519 cal/100g with 33g fat. Air-dried banana = zero fat, same crunch. Night and day difference.
400 cal/100g but 40 cal per bag. Single ingredient, nothing added. Popular in school lunches. Much better than fruit snacks at similar price point.
333 cal/100g but only 25 cal per bag! Among the lowest-calorie packaged fruit snacks you can buy. Just strawberries, nothing else.

321 cal/100g, 64g sugar/100g. One strip = 45 cal. Not terrible for a single strip, but the sugar density is high. Made from fruit puree concentrate — essentially concentrated fruit sugar pressed flat.
321 cal/100g, 64g sugar/100g. Same calorie profile as other flavors. No fiber in grape version. All flavors are essentially identical nutritionally.
BASELINE JUNK. 320 cal/100g, 44g sugar/100g. Fruit snacks are candy marketed to kids as fruit. Corn syrup is the first ingredient. Zero fiber, zero nutrition. The 'fruit' is just flavoring.
BASELINE JUNK. 354 cal/100g, 44g sugar/100g. Same as Welch's — candy in a fruit-shaped disguise. Zero fiber. Parents pack these in lunchboxes thinking it's a fruit serving.
304 cal/100g, 48g sugar/100g. 'Organic' doesn't mean healthy — still sugar-based with zero fiber. Slightly less cal than Welch's but same category: candy with an organic label.
308 cal/100g, 46g sugar/100g. Claims 'made with real fruit juice' but still corn syrup-based candy. Same category as Welch's and Mott's.
350 cal/100g, 50g sugar/100g. 'Organic, no artificial anything' — but still pure sugar gummies. No fiber, no protein. Marginally better ingredient list than Welch's but nutritionally identical.

300 cal/100g, 50g sugar/100g. THE 'healthy' fruit snack — just real fruit, no added sugar. Better ingredient list than competitors BUT still 50% sugar by weight. One roll = fine. Three rolls = candy bar equivalent.

348 cal/100g, 61g sugar/100g. Single ingredient — just organic mango, dried flat. No added sugar. Better than fruit snack gummies but still concentrated fruit sugar. The jerky-like texture makes you eat more slowly.
348 cal/100g, 61g sugar/100g. Same profile as mango — just dried pineapple, nothing added. Decent portable snack if you stick to one strip.
348 cal/100g, 52g sugar/100g. Banana version has slightly less sugar and more fiber than mango/pineapple. Still 0g fat — compare to fried banana chips at 33g fat/100g.

350 cal/100g, 65g sugar/100g. No added sugar but still calorie-dense. The 'organic, no added sugar' label makes people eat more thinking it's guilt-free. Still 5.8x more calorie-dense than fresh mango.
400 cal/100g, 67g sugar/100g on paper BUT the serving is tiny and light. 60 cal per snack bag. Baked, not fried. Just apples, nothing else. Good swap for chips.
393 cal/100g, 0g fat. CRITICAL COMPARISON: Bare baked banana = 0g fat, 393 cal/100g. Generic FRIED banana chips = 33g fat, 519 cal/100g. Same fruit, totally different product. Always choose baked.
325 cal/100g, 55g sugar/100g. No added sugar, organic. Slightly better than Philippine Brand (375 cal/100g with added sugar). The 'gently dried' process preserves more nutrients.
357 cal/100g, 57g sugar/100g. Unique — dries fruit WITH the skin, adding fiber other brands lose. Strawberry + pear blend. No added sugar. Higher fiber than competitors but still calorie-dense.

536 cal/100g, 32g fat/100g. COMPARISON PRODUCT — veggie chips are NOT healthier than regular chips. Same calories, same fat. The 'sweet potato' label is a health halo. Regular Lay's = 536 cal/100g too.

467 cal/100g but only 70 cal per bag. Just pineapple, air-dried. Higher sugar than apple/strawberry versions because pineapple is naturally sweeter. Still way better than dried pineapple rings which are often sweetened.
500 cal/100g on paper but only 50 cal per bag. Just strawberries. The volume-to-weight ratio is the key — a full bag feels like a real snack for 50 cal.

400 cal/100g but 60 cal per serving. Freeze-dried pineapple = light, crunchy, 60 cal. Regular dried pineapple rings = dense, chewy, 130 cal per similar visual portion. Same fruit, dramatically different eating experience.
